ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for a customisable multi-topping grain bowl; FOODON_00002473 (grain-based dish) used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (grain-based composite dish) is the closest defensible category; no exact code exists for a customisable grain bowl with variable toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to a customisable grain bowl food item; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: cooked grains (e.g. brown rice, quinoa, farro)
assorted toppings (e.g. roasted vegetables, legumes, protein, leafy greens)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard grain bowl with four toppings and dressing weighs approximately 350 g, representing a typical single-serve lunch or dinner portion as sold at health-casual food stalls and cafes in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Grain Bowl (4 Toppings) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Grain Bowl (4 Toppings), CR ID F08010030. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated at ~1.0 g/100g based on typical grain bowl fat composition (predominantly unsaturated from vegetable toppings and dressing) using USDA FoodData Central composite grain bowl analogues as a cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed grain and rice bowl analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a customisable 4-topping grain bowl. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous mixed grain dishes (brown rice and mixed grain bowls) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 48–60 for similar grain-based composite dishes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (12.44 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) = 55 × 13.8 / 100 ≈ 7.6 g GL per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 229 mg/100g; approximately 802 mg per 350 g serving
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving contributes approximately 802 mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; request dressing on the side and choose low-sodium sauce options to reduce sodium load meaningfully.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~55), low glycemic load per serving (~7.6 g), and dietary fibre at 1.98 g/100g moderating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated low GI of 55 combined with a low glycemic load of 7.6 g per serving makes this bowl suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management; selecting whole-grain bases (brown rice, quinoa) and avoiding sweet dressings will further lower the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.0 g/100g), moderate dietary fibre (1.98 g/100g), and plant-forward topping composition
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content (~1.0 g/100g) and plant-based toppings support a heart-healthy dietary pattern; however, sodium at 229 mg/100g warrants attention for individuals with concurrent hypertension and CVD, and sauce selection should prioritise low-sodium, unsaturated-fat-based dressings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.0 g/100g), dietary fibre (1.98 g/100g), and potential antioxidant contribution from vegetable toppings
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The low saturated fat and fibre-containing grain base are consistent with an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern; individuals with active RA should specify toppings to avoid nightshade vegetables if symptomatic and favour anti-inflammatory additions such as leafy greens, turmeric, or ginger.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A grain bowl with four toppings is a customisable, health-oriented mixed dish popular at health-casual food stalls, cafes, and fast-casual restaurants in Singapore. The base consists of cooked grains — commonly brown rice, white rice, quinoa, farro, or a multigrain blend — topped with four customer-selected items that typically include a combination of roasted or raw vegetables, legumes (chickpeas, edamame, lentils), a protein source (grilled chicken, tofu, egg, or fish), and leafy greens, finished with a dressing or sauce. The dish is assembled rather than cooked as a single unit, meaning its nutritional profile varies with topping and dressing choices. Per 100 g, the HPB Foodo database reports 108 kcal, 3.15 g protein, 5.08 g fat, 12.44 g carbohydrate, 3.76 g sugar, 1.98 g dietary fibre, and 229 mg sodium. Calcium is 19 mg/100g and iron is 0.87 mg/100g. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 55 (low), yielding a low glycemic load of about 7.6 g per standard 350 g serving. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI and low GL; it is broadly heart-healthy due to low saturated fat; however, sodium at 229 mg/100g is borderline for hypertensive individuals and sauce selection is the primary lever for sodium control. The dish is generally halal-compatible and can be made vegetarian or vegan depending on topping choices. It is classified as minimally processed and is a common lunch or dinner option in Singapore's health-conscious dining scene.
common_user_queries: Is a grain bowl healthy for diabetics?
How many calories in a grain bowl with 4 toppings?
Is grain bowl good for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of a grain bowl?
Best grain bowl toppings for weight loss
Grain bowl vs rice bowl calories

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (CR ID F08010030) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Saturated fat estimated at ~1.0 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central grain bowl analogues; GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous mixed grain dish entries. All primary nutrient data are from an approved primary regional source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item (F08010030) and no equivalent approved secondary source provides complete fatty acid fractionation or full micronutrient panel for this specific customisable dish. Saturated_fat_g is estimated at 1.0 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central composite grain bowl analogues; this is an approximation and should be updated when HPB publishes a full fatty acid breakdown. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mixed grain bowl entries; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the approved GI source database. Dietary flags (is_vegetarian: true, is_halal: true) reflect the most common preparation; actual suitability depends on topping and dressing selection by the customer.
